obj.title: Maryam Mirzakhani
obj.content:
Maryam Mirzakhani is regarded as [match]27[/match] in the field of mathematics because she was the only female holder of the prestigious Fields Medal –a record that she retained at the time of her death. However, maths held little  [match]28[/match] for her as a child and in fact her performance was below  average until she was [match]29[/match] by a difficult puzzle that one of her siblings showed her.[br][br]

Later, as a professional mathematician, she had an inquiring mind and proved herself to be [match]30[/match] when things did not go smoothly. She said she got the greatest [match]31[/match] from making ground-breaking discoveries and in fact she was responsible for some extremely [match]32[/match] mathematical studies.
obj.html_content:
Maryam Mirzakhani is regarded as 27 in the field of mathematics because she was the only female holder of the prestigious Fields Medal –a record that she retained at the time of her death. However, maths held little 28 for her as a child and in fact her performance was below average until she was 29 by a difficult puzzle that one of her siblings showed her.

Later, as a professional mathematician, she had an inquiring mind and proved herself to be 30 when things did not go smoothly. She said she got the greatest 31 from making ground-breaking discoveries and in fact she was responsible for some extremely 32 mathematical studies.

obj.title: An inquiry into the existence of the gifted child
obj.content:
[br/]
Let us start by looking at a modern 'genius', Maryam Mirzakhani, who died at the early age of 40. She was the only woman to win the Fields Medal – the mathematical equivalent of a Nobel prize. It would be easy to assume that someone as special as Mirzakhani must have been one of those 'gifted' children, those who have an extraordinary ability in a specific sphere of activity or knowledge. But look closer and a different story emerges. Mirzakhani was born in Tehran, Iran. She went to a highly selective girls' school but maths wasn't her interest – reading was. She loved novels and would read anything she could lay her hands on. As for maths, she did rather poorly at it for the first couple of years in her middle school, but became interested when her elder brother told her about what he'd learned. He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her – and she was hooked.[br/][br/]
In adult life it is clear that she was curious, excited by what she did and also resolute in the face of setbacks. One of her comments sums it up. 'Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new . . . But most of the time, doing mathematics for me is like being on a long hike with no trail and no end in sight.' That trail took her to the heights of original research into mathematics.[br/][br/]
Is her background unusual? Apparently not. Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood. Einstein was slow to talk as a baby. He failed the general part of the entry test to Zurich Polytechnic – though they let him in because of high physics and maths scores. He struggled at work initially, but he kept plugging away and eventually rewrote the laws of Newtonian mechanics with his theory of relativity.[br/][br/]
There has been a considerable amount of research on high performance over the last century that suggests it goes way beyond tested intelligence. On top of that, research is clear that brains are flexible, new neural pathways can be created, and IQ isn't fixed. For example, just because you can read stories with hundreds of pages at the age of five doesn't mean you will still be ahead of your contemporaries in your teens. [br/][br/]
While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference,  what is certain is that the behaviours associated with high levels of performance are replicable and most can be taught – even traits such as curiosity.[br/][br/]
According to my colleague Prof Deborah Eyre, with whom I've collaborated on the book Great Minds and How to Grow Them, the latest neuroscience and psychological research suggests most individuals can reach levels of performance associated in school with the gifted and talented. However, they must be taught the right attitudes and approaches to their learning and develop the attributes of high performers – curiosity, persistence and hard work, for example – an approach Eyre calls 'high performance learning'. Critically, they need the right support in developing those approaches at home as well as at school.[br/][br/]
Prof Anders Ericsson, an eminent education psychologist at Florida State University, US, is the co-author of Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise. After research going back to 1980 into diverse achievements, from music to memory to sport, he doesn't think unique and innate talents are at the heart of performance. Deliberate practice, that stretches you every step of the way, and around 10,000 hours of it, is what produces the goods. It's not a magic number – the highest performers move on to doing a whole lot more, of course. Ericsson's memory research is particularly interesting because random students, trained in memory techniques for the study, went on to outperform others thought to have innately superior memories – those who you might call gifted.[br/][br/]
But it is perhaps the work of Benjamin Bloom, another distinguished American educationist working in the 1980s, that gives the most pause for thought. Bloom's team looked at a group of extraordinarily high achieving people in disciplines as varied as ballet, swimming, piano, tennis, maths, sculpture and neurology. He found a pattern of parents encouraging and supporting their children, often in areas they enjoyed themselves. Bloom's outstanding people had worked very hard and consistently at something they had become hooked on when at a young age, and their parents all emerged as having strong work ethics themselves.[br/][br/]
Eyre says we know how high performers learn. From that she has developed a high performing learning approach. She is working on this with a group of schools, both in Britain and abroad. Some spin-off research, which looked in detail at 24 of the 3,000 children being studied who were succeeding despite difficult circumstances, found something remarkable. Half were getting free school meals because of poverty, more than half were living with a single parent, and four in five were living in disadvantaged areas. Interviews uncovered strong evidence of an adult or adults in the child's life who valued and supported education, either in the immediate or extended family or in the child's wider community. Children talked about the need to work hard at school, to listen in class and keep trying.[br/][br/]
Let us end with Einstein, the epitome of a genius. He clearly had curiosity, character and determination. He struggled against rejection in early life but was undeterred. Did he think he was a genius or even gifted? He once wrote: 'It's not that I'm so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character.'
obj.content_zh:
[br/]
让我们先来看一位现代的“天才”，玛丽安·米尔扎哈尼，她去世时才40岁。她是唯一一位获得菲尔兹奖的女性，这是数学上的诺贝尔奖。人们很容易认为，像米尔扎哈尼这样特别的人一定是那些“天才”孩子中的一员，他们在特定的活动或知识领域拥有非凡的能力。但仔细观察，就会发现一个不同的故事。米尔扎哈尼出生于伊朗德黑兰。她上的是一所精挑细选的女子学校，但她的兴趣不是数学，而是阅读。她喜欢小说，能找到什么就读什么。至于数学，在她上中学的头几年里，她学得很差，但当她哥哥告诉她他学到的东西时，她开始感兴趣了。他分享了一本杂志上的一个著名的数学问题，让她着迷——她被迷住了。[br/][br/]
在成年后的生活中，很明显，她对自己所做的事情充满好奇和兴奋，面对挫折也很坚决。她的一条评论总结了这一点。当然，最有意义的部分是“啊哈”的时刻，发现新事物的兴奋和理解新事物的享受……但大多数时候，做数学对我来说就像一次没有路也看不到尽头的长途徒步旅行。”这条路把她带到了数学原创性研究的高度。[br/][br/]
她的背景不寻常吗?显然不是。大多数诺贝尔奖得主的童年都很平凡。爱因斯坦小时候说话很慢。他在苏黎世理工大学的入学考试中没有通过普通考试，但由于他的物理和数学成绩很高，他被录取了。他最初在工作中遇到了困难，但他坚持不懈，最终用他的相对论重写了牛顿力学定律。[br/][br/]
在过去的一个世纪里，有大量关于高绩效的研究表明，它远远超出了测试智力的范畴。最重要的是，研究清楚地表明，大脑是灵活的，可以创造新的神经通路，智商也不是固定的。例如，仅仅因为你在五岁的时候能读几百页的故事，并不意味着你在十几岁的时候仍然领先于同龄人。[br/][br/]
虽然目前还不清楚天赋是天生的，还是其他因素可能造成了差异，但可以肯定的是，与高水平表现相关的行为是可以复制的，而且大多数是可以教授的——甚至包括好奇心这样的特征。据我的同事黛博拉·艾尔教授(Deborah Eyre)说，最新的神经科学和心理学研究表明，大多数人在学校的表现都能达到与天才和天才相关的水平。我和黛博拉·艾尔教授合著了《伟大的心灵和如何培养它们》(Great Minds and How to Grow Them)一书。然而，他们必须被教导正确的学习态度和方法，并培养优秀学生的特质——例如好奇心、毅力和勤奋——艾尔称之为“高效学习”的方法。至关重要的是，他们需要在家庭和学校发展这些方法的正确支持。[br/][br/]
据我的同事黛博拉·艾尔教授(Deborah Eyre)说，最新的神经科学和心理学研究表明，大多数人在学校的表现都能达到与天才和天才相关的水平。我和黛博拉·艾尔教授合著了《伟大的心灵和如何培养它们》(Great Minds and How to Grow Them)一书。然而，他们必须被教导正确的学习态度和方法，并培养优秀学生的特质——例如好奇心、毅力和勤奋——艾尔称之为“高效学习”的方法。至关重要的是，他们需要在家庭和学校发展这些方法的正确支持。[br/][br/]
美国佛罗里达州立大学著名教育心理学家安德斯·埃里克森教授是《顶峰:新科学的秘密》一书的合著者。从1980年开始，他研究了从音乐到记忆再到体育的各种成就，他认为表演的核心并不是独特和天生的才能。刻意练习，每一步都让你伸展，大约一万个小时的练习，才会产生好的效果。这不是一个神奇的数字——当然，表现最好的人会继续做更多的事情。埃里克森的记忆研究特别有趣，因为随机挑选的学生，在研究中接受了记忆技巧的训练，他们的表现比那些被认为天生记忆力出众的人——那些你可能称之为天才的人——要好得多。[br/][br/]
但或许是另一位杰出的美国教育家本杰明•布鲁姆(Benjamin Bloom)在20世纪80年代的研究成果，最让人深思。布鲁姆的研究小组研究了一组在芭蕾、游泳、钢琴、网球、数学、雕塑和神经学等不同学科中取得非凡成就的人。他发现了一种父母鼓励和支持孩子的模式，通常是在他们喜欢的领域。布鲁姆的优秀员工在年轻时就对某件事着迷，他们一直非常努力地工作，他们的父母也都有很强的职业道德。[br/][br/]
艾尔说我们知道高绩效者是如何学习的。由此，她发展出了一种高效的学习方法。她正在与英国和国外的一些学校合作。一些衍生研究详细观察了3000名在困难环境下取得成功的儿童中的24名，发现了一些值得注意的事情。其中一半的孩子因为贫困而获得免费校餐，一半以上的孩子与单亲父母生活在一起，五分之四的孩子生活在贫困地区。采访发现了强有力的证据，表明在孩子的生活中有一个或多个成年人重视和支持教育，无论是在直系亲属或大家庭中，还是在孩子更广泛的社区中。孩子们谈到了在学校努力学习、听课和不断尝试的必要性。[br/][br/]
让我们以天才的缩影爱因斯坦作为结束。他显然有好奇心、个性和决心。他早年曾与拒绝作斗争，但没有被吓倒。他认为自己是个天才，甚至有天赋吗?他曾写道:“这并不是说我有多聪明，只是我能更长久地面对问题。”大多数人说，是智力造就了伟大的科学家。他们错了，这是性格。”
obj.html_content:
Let us start by looking at a modern 'genius', Maryam Mirzakhani, who died at the early age of 40. She was the only woman to win the Fields Medal – the mathematical equivalent of a Nobel prize. It would be easy to assume that someone as special as Mirzakhani must have been one of those 'gifted' children, those who have an extraordinary ability in a specific sphere of activity or knowledge. But look closer and a different story emerges. Mirzakhani was born in Tehran, Iran. She went to a highly selective girls' school but maths wasn't her interest – reading was. She loved novels and would read anything she could lay her hands on. As for maths, she did rather poorly at it for the first couple of years in her middle school, but became interested when her elder brother told her about what he'd learned. He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her – and she was hooked.



In adult life it is clear that she was curious, excited by what she did and also resolute in the face of setbacks. One of her comments sums it up. 'Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new . . . But most of the time, doing mathematics for me is like being on a long hike with no trail and no end in sight.' That trail took her to the heights of original research into mathematics.



Is her background unusual? Apparently not. Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood. Einstein was slow to talk as a baby. He failed the general part of the entry test to Zurich Polytechnic – though they let him in because of high physics and maths scores. He struggled at work initially, but he kept plugging away and eventually rewrote the laws of Newtonian mechanics with his theory of relativity.



There has been a considerable amount of research on high performance over the last century that suggests it goes way beyond tested intelligence. On top of that, research is clear that brains are flexible, new neural pathways can be created, and IQ isn't fixed. For example, just because you can read stories with hundreds of pages at the age of five doesn't mean you will still be ahead of your contemporaries in your teens.



While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference, what is certain is that the behaviours associated with high levels of performance are replicable and most can be taught – even traits such as curiosity.



According to my colleague Prof Deborah Eyre, with whom I've collaborated on the book Great Minds and How to Grow Them, the latest neuroscience and psychological research suggests most individuals can reach levels of performance associated in school with the gifted and talented. However, they must be taught the right attitudes and approaches to their learning and develop the attributes of high performers – curiosity, persistence and hard work, for example – an approach Eyre calls 'high performance learning'. Critically, they need the right support in developing those approaches at home as well as at school.



Prof Anders Ericsson, an eminent education psychologist at Florida State University, US, is the co-author of Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise. After research going back to 1980 into diverse achievements, from music to memory to sport, he doesn't think unique and innate talents are at the heart of performance. Deliberate practice, that stretches you every step of the way, and around 10,000 hours of it, is what produces the goods. It's not a magic number – the highest performers move on to doing a whole lot more, of course. Ericsson's memory research is particularly interesting because random students, trained in memory techniques for the study, went on to outperform others thought to have innately superior memories – those who you might call gifted.



But it is perhaps the work of Benjamin Bloom, another distinguished American educationist working in the 1980s, that gives the most pause for thought. Bloom's team looked at a group of extraordinarily high achieving people in disciplines as varied as ballet, swimming, piano, tennis, maths, sculpture and neurology. He found a pattern of parents encouraging and supporting their children, often in areas they enjoyed themselves. Bloom's outstanding people had worked very hard and consistently at something they had become hooked on when at a young age, and their parents all emerged as having strong work ethics themselves.



Eyre says we know how high performers learn. From that she has developed a high performing learning approach. She is working on this with a group of schools, both in Britain and abroad. Some spin-off research, which looked in detail at 24 of the 3,000 children being studied who were succeeding despite difficult circumstances, found something remarkable. Half were getting free school meals because of poverty, more than half were living with a single parent, and four in five were living in disadvantaged areas. Interviews uncovered strong evidence of an adult or adults in the child's life who valued and supported education, either in the immediate or extended family or in the child's wider community. Children talked about the need to work hard at school, to listen in class and keep trying.



Let us end with Einstein, the epitome of a genius. He clearly had curiosity, character and determination. He struggled against rejection in early life but was undeterred. Did he think he was a genius or even gifted? He once wrote: 'It's not that I'm so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character.'

obj.content: Many people who ended up winning prestigious intellectual prizes only reached an average standard when young.
obj.html_content: Many people who ended up winning prestigious intellectual prizes only reached an average standard when young.

obj.content: Einstein's failures as a young man were due to his lack of confidence.

obj.content: It is difficult to reach agreement on whether some children are actually born gifted.

obj.content: Einstein was upset by the public's view of his life's work.

obj.content: Einstein put his success down to the speed at which he dealt with scientific questions.

obj.title: An inquiry into the existence of the gifted child

她的背景不寻常吗?显然不是。大多数诺贝尔奖得主的童年都很平凡。爱因斯坦小时候说话很慢。他在苏黎世理工大学的入学考试中没有通过普通考试，但由于他的物理和数学成绩很高，他被录取了。他最初在工作中遇到了困难，但他坚持不懈，最终用他的相对论重写了牛顿力学定律。[br/][br/]
在过去的一个世纪里，有大量关于高绩效的研究表明，它远远超出了测试智力的范畴。最重要的是，研究清楚地表明，大脑是灵活的，可以创造新的神经通路，智商也不是固定的。例如，仅仅因为你在五岁的时候能读几百页的故事，并不意味着你在十几岁的时候仍然领先于同龄人。[br/][br/]
虽然目前还不清楚天赋是天生的，还是其他因素可能造成了差异，但可以肯定的是，与高水平表现相关的行为是可以复制的，而且大多数是可以教授的——甚至包括好奇心这样的特征。据我的同事黛博拉·艾尔教授(Deborah Eyre)说，最新的神经科学和心理学研究表明，大多数人在学校的表现都能达到与天才和天才相关的水平。我和黛博拉·艾尔教授合著了《伟大的心灵和如何培养它们》(Great Minds and How to Grow Them)一书。然而，他们必须被教导正确的学习态度和方法，并培养优秀学生的特质——例如好奇心、毅力和勤奋——艾尔称之为“高效学习”的方法。至关重要的是，他们需要在家庭和学校发展这些方法的正确支持。[br/][br/]
据我的同事黛博拉·艾尔教授(Deborah Eyre)说，最新的神经科学和心理学研究表明，大多数人在学校的表现都能达到与天才和天才相关的水平。我和黛博拉·艾尔教授合著了《伟大的心灵和如何培养它们》(Great Minds and How to Grow Them)一书。然而，他们必须被教导正确的学习态度和方法，并培养优秀学生的特质——例如好奇心、毅力和勤奋——艾尔称之为“高效学习”的方法。至关重要的是，他们需要在家庭和学校发展这些方法的正确支持。[br/][br/]
美国佛罗里达州立大学著名教育心理学家安德斯·埃里克森教授是《顶峰:新科学的秘密》一书的合著者。从1980年开始，他研究了从音乐到记忆再到体育的各种成就，他认为表演的核心并不是独特和天生的才能。刻意练习，每一步都让你伸展，大约一万个小时的练习，才会产生好的效果。这不是一个神奇的数字——当然，表现最好的人会继续做更多的事情。埃里克森的记忆研究特别有趣，因为随机挑选的学生，在研究中接受了记忆技巧的训练，他们的表现比那些被认为天生记忆力出众的人——那些你可能称之为天才的人——要好得多。[br/][br/]
但或许是另一位杰出的美国教育家本杰明•布鲁姆(Benjamin Bloom)在20世纪80年代的研究成果，最让人深思。布鲁姆的研究小组研究了一组在芭蕾、游泳、钢琴、网球、数学、雕塑和神经学等不同学科中取得非凡成就的人。他发现了一种父母鼓励和支持孩子的模式，通常是在他们喜欢的领域。布鲁姆的优秀员工在年轻时就对某件事着迷，他们一直非常努力地工作，他们的父母也都有很强的职业道德。[br/][br/]
艾尔说我们知道高绩效者是如何学习的。由此，她发展出了一种高效的学习方法。她正在与英国和国外的一些学校合作。一些衍生研究详细观察了3000名在困难环境下取得成功的儿童中的24名，发现了一些值得注意的事情。其中一半的孩子因为贫困而获得免费校餐，一半以上的孩子与单亲父母生活在一起，五分之四的孩子生活在贫困地区。采访发现了强有力的证据，表明在孩子的生活中有一个或多个成年人重视和支持教育，无论是在直系亲属或大家庭中，还是在孩子更广泛的社区中。孩子们谈到了在学校努力学习、听课和不断尝试的必要性。[br/][br/]
让我们以天才的缩影爱因斯坦作为结束。他显然有好奇心、个性和决心。他早年曾与拒绝作斗争，但没有被吓倒。他认为自己是个天才，甚至有天赋吗?他曾写道:“这并不是说我有多聪明，只是我能更长久地面对问题。”大多数人说，是智力造就了伟大的科学家。他们错了，这是性格。”
obj.html_content:
Let us start by looking at a modern 'genius', Maryam Mirzakhani, who died at the early age of 40. She was the only woman to win the Fields Medal – the mathematical equivalent of a Nobel prize. It would be easy to assume that someone as special as Mirzakhani must have been one of those 'gifted' children, those who have an extraordinary ability in a specific sphere of activity or knowledge. But look closer and a different story emerges. Mirzakhani was born in Tehran, Iran. She went to a highly selective girls' school but maths wasn't her interest – reading was. She loved novels and would read anything she could lay her hands on. As for maths, she did rather poorly at it for the first couple of years in her middle school, but became interested when her elder brother told her about what he'd learned. He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her – and she was hooked.



In adult life it is clear that she was curious, excited by what she did and also resolute in the face of setbacks. One of her comments sums it up. 'Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new . . . But most of the time, doing mathematics for me is like being on a long hike with no trail and no end in sight.' That trail took her to the heights of original research into mathematics.



Is her background unusual? Apparently not. Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood. Einstein was slow to talk as a baby. He failed the general part of the entry test to Zurich Polytechnic – though they let him in because of high physics and maths scores. He struggled at work initially, but he kept plugging away and eventually rewrote the laws of Newtonian mechanics with his theory of relativity.



There has been a considerable amount of research on high performance over the last century that suggests it goes way beyond tested intelligence. On top of that, research is clear that brains are flexible, new neural pathways can be created, and IQ isn't fixed. For example, just because you can read stories with hundreds of pages at the age of five doesn't mean you will still be ahead of your contemporaries in your teens.



While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference, what is certain is that the behaviours associated with high levels of performance are replicable and most can be taught – even traits such as curiosity.



According to my colleague Prof Deborah Eyre, with whom I've collaborated on the book Great Minds and How to Grow Them, the latest neuroscience and psychological research suggests most individuals can reach levels of performance associated in school with the gifted and talented. However, they must be taught the right attitudes and approaches to their learning and develop the attributes of high performers – curiosity, persistence and hard work, for example – an approach Eyre calls 'high performance learning'. Critically, they need the right support in developing those approaches at home as well as at school.



Prof Anders Ericsson, an eminent education psychologist at Florida State University, US, is the co-author of Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise. After research going back to 1980 into diverse achievements, from music to memory to sport, he doesn't think unique and innate talents are at the heart of performance. Deliberate practice, that stretches you every step of the way, and around 10,000 hours of it, is what produces the goods. It's not a magic number – the highest performers move on to doing a whole lot more, of course. Ericsson's memory research is particularly interesting because random students, trained in memory techniques for the study, went on to outperform others thought to have innately superior memories – those who you might call gifted.



But it is perhaps the work of Benjamin Bloom, another distinguished American educationist working in the 1980s, that gives the most pause for thought. Bloom's team looked at a group of extraordinarily high achieving people in disciplines as varied as ballet, swimming, piano, tennis, maths, sculpture and neurology. He found a pattern of parents encouraging and supporting their children, often in areas they enjoyed themselves. Bloom's outstanding people had worked very hard and consistently at something they had become hooked on when at a young age, and their parents all emerged as having strong work ethics themselves.



Eyre says we know how high performers learn. From that she has developed a high performing learning approach. She is working on this with a group of schools, both in Britain and abroad. Some spin-off research, which looked in detail at 24 of the 3,000 children being studied who were succeeding despite difficult circumstances, found something remarkable. Half were getting free school meals because of poverty, more than half were living with a single parent, and four in five were living in disadvantaged areas. Interviews uncovered strong evidence of an adult or adults in the child's life who valued and supported education, either in the immediate or extended family or in the child's wider community. Children talked about the need to work hard at school, to listen in class and keep trying.



Let us end with Einstein, the epitome of a genius. He clearly had curiosity, character and determination. He struggled against rejection in early life but was undeterred. Did he think he was a genius or even gifted? He once wrote: 'It's not that I'm so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character.'

A
,

,

or

obj.content: What does Eyre believe is needed for children to equal 'gifted' standards?

obj.html_content: strict discipline from the teaching staff

obj.content: assistance from their peers in the classroom
obj.html_content: assistance from their peers in the classroom

obj.content: the development of a spirit of inquiry towards their studies
obj.html_content: the development of a spirit of inquiry towards their studies

obj.html_content: the determination to surpass everyone else's achievements

obj.html_content: What is the result of Ericsson's research?

obj.content: Very gifted students do not need to work on improving memory skills.
obj.html_content: Very gifted students do not need to work on improving memory skills.

obj.content: Being born with a special gift is not the key factor in becoming expert.
obj.html_content: Being born with a special gift is not the key factor in becoming expert.

obj.content: Including time for physical exercise is crucial in raising performance.
obj.html_content: Including time for physical exercise is crucial in raising performance.

obj.content: 10,000 hours of relevant and demanding work will create a genius.
obj.html_content: 10,000 hours of relevant and demanding work will create a genius.

obj.html_content: In the penultimate paragraph, it is stated the key to some deprived children's success is

obj.html_content: the loving support of more than one parent.

obj.title: An inquiry into the existence of the gifted child

obj.content_zh:
[br/]
让我们先来看一位现代的“天才”，玛丽安·米尔扎哈尼，她去世时才40岁。她是唯一一位获得菲尔兹奖的女性，这是数学上的诺贝尔奖。人们很容易认为，像米尔扎哈尼这样特别的人一定是那些“天才”孩子中的一员，他们在特定的活动或知识领域拥有非凡的能力。但仔细观察，就会发现一个不同的故事。米尔扎哈尼出生于伊朗德黑兰。她上的是一所精挑细选的女子学校，但她的兴趣不是数学，而是阅读。她喜欢小说，能找到什么就读什么。至于数学，在她上中学的头几年里，她学得很差，但当她哥哥告诉她他学到的东西时，她开始感兴趣了。他分享了一本杂志上的一个著名的数学问题，让她着迷——她被迷住了。[br/][br/]
在成年后的生活中，很明显，她对自己所做的事情充满好奇和兴奋，面对挫折也很坚决。她的一条评论总结了这一点。当然，最有意义的部分是“啊哈”的时刻，发现新事物的兴奋和理解新事物的享受……但大多数时候，做数学对我来说就像一次没有路也看不到尽头的长途徒步旅行。”这条路把她带到了数学原创性研究的高度。[br/][br/]
她的背景不寻常吗?显然不是。大多数诺贝尔奖得主的童年都很平凡。爱因斯坦小时候说话很慢。他在苏黎世理工大学的入学考试中没有通过普通考试，但由于他的物理和数学成绩很高，他被录取了。他最初在工作中遇到了困难，但他坚持不懈，最终用他的相对论重写了牛顿力学定律。[br/][br/]
在过去的一个世纪里，有大量关于高绩效的研究表明，它远远超出了测试智力的范畴。最重要的是，研究清楚地表明，大脑是灵活的，可以创造新的神经通路，智商也不是固定的。例如，仅仅因为你在五岁的时候能读几百页的故事，并不意味着你在十几岁的时候仍然领先于同龄人。[br/][br/]
虽然目前还不清楚天赋是天生的，还是其他因素可能造成了差异，但可以肯定的是，与高水平表现相关的行为是可以复制的，而且大多数是可以教授的——甚至包括好奇心这样的特征。据我的同事黛博拉·艾尔教授(Deborah Eyre)说，最新的神经科学和心理学研究表明，大多数人在学校的表现都能达到与天才和天才相关的水平。我和黛博拉·艾尔教授合著了《伟大的心灵和如何培养它们》(Great Minds and How to Grow Them)一书。然而，他们必须被教导正确的学习态度和方法，并培养优秀学生的特质——例如好奇心、毅力和勤奋——艾尔称之为“高效学习”的方法。至关重要的是，他们需要在家庭和学校发展这些方法的正确支持。[br/][br/]
据我的同事黛博拉·艾尔教授(Deborah Eyre)说，最新的神经科学和心理学研究表明，大多数人在学校的表现都能达到与天才和天才相关的水平。我和黛博拉·艾尔教授合著了《伟大的心灵和如何培养它们》(Great Minds and How to Grow Them)一书。然而，他们必须被教导正确的学习态度和方法，并培养优秀学生的特质——例如好奇心、毅力和勤奋——艾尔称之为“高效学习”的方法。至关重要的是，他们需要在家庭和学校发展这些方法的正确支持。[br/][br/]
美国佛罗里达州立大学著名教育心理学家安德斯·埃里克森教授是《顶峰:新科学的秘密》一书的合著者。从1980年开始，他研究了从音乐到记忆再到体育的各种成就，他认为表演的核心并不是独特和天生的才能。刻意练习，每一步都让你伸展，大约一万个小时的练习，才会产生好的效果。这不是一个神奇的数字——当然，表现最好的人会继续做更多的事情。埃里克森的记忆研究特别有趣，因为随机挑选的学生，在研究中接受了记忆技巧的训练，他们的表现比那些被认为天生记忆力出众的人——那些你可能称之为天才的人——要好得多。[br/][br/]

He failed the general part of the entry test to Zurich Polytechnic – though they let him in because of high physics and maths scores. He struggled at work initially, but he kept plugging away and eventually rewrote the laws of Newtonian mechanics with his theory of relativity.



There has been a considerable amount of research on high performance over the last century that suggests it goes way beyond tested intelligence. On top of that, research is clear that brains are flexible, new neural pathways can be created, and IQ isn't fixed. For example, just because you can read stories with hundreds of pages at the age of five doesn't mean you will still be ahead of your contemporaries in your teens.



While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference, what is certain is that the behaviours associated with high levels of performance are replicable and most can be taught – even traits such as curiosity.



According to my colleague Prof Deborah Eyre, with whom I've collaborated on the book Great Minds and How to Grow Them, the latest neuroscience and psychological research suggests most individuals can reach levels of performance associated in school with the gifted and talented. However, they must be taught the right attitudes and approaches to their learning and develop the attributes of high performers – curiosity, persistence and hard work, for example – an approach Eyre calls 'high performance learning'. Critically, they need the right support in developing those approaches at home as well as at school.



Prof Anders Ericsson, an eminent education psychologist at Florida State University, US, is the co-author of Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise. After research going back to 1980 into diverse achievements, from music to memory to sport, he doesn't think unique and innate talents are at the heart of performance. Deliberate practice, that stretches you every step of the way, and around 10,000 hours of it, is what produces the goods. It's not a magic number – the highest performers move on to doing a whole lot more, of course. Ericsson's memory research is particularly interesting because random students, trained in memory techniques for the study, went on to outperform others thought to have innately superior memories – those who you might call gifted.



But it is perhaps the work of Benjamin Bloom, another distinguished American educationist working in the 1980s, that gives the most pause for thought. Bloom's team looked at a group of extraordinarily high achieving people in disciplines as varied as ballet, swimming, piano, tennis, maths, sculpture and neurology. He found a pattern of parents encouraging and supporting their children, often in areas they enjoyed themselves. Bloom's outstanding people had worked very hard and consistently at something they had become hooked on when at a young age, and their parents all emerged as having strong work ethics themselves.



Eyre says we know how high performers learn. From that she has developed a high performing learning approach. She is working on this with a group of schools, both in Britain and abroad. Some spin-off research, which looked in detail at 24 of the 3,000 children being studied who were succeeding despite difficult circumstances, found something remarkable. Half were getting free school meals because of poverty, more than half were living with a single parent, and four in five were living in disadvantaged areas. Interviews uncovered strong evidence of an adult or adults in the child's life who valued and supported education, either in the immediate or extended family or in the child's wider community. Children talked about the need to work hard at school, to listen in class and keep trying.



Let us end with Einstein, the epitome of a genius. He clearly had curiosity, character and determination. He struggled against rejection in early life but was undeterred. Did he think he was a genius or even gifted? He once wrote: 'It's not that I'm so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character.'

sectionSheet.title: An inquiry into the existence of the gifted child

officialExplain.explain:
译文：Maryam Mirzakhani在数学领域中被认为是______，因为她是唯一一位持有著名Fields Medal奖的女性——这个记录在她去世时依然无人打破。

本题的定位非常简单：依靠首字母大写的人名可以直接锁定第一段第一句话，但这句话里并没有空格内所需信息；不过这也无妨，依据另外一个大写信息Fields Medal可以很快锁定接下来的第二句。然而，本题的解答其实不太容易，因为考生需要首先读懂题干、判断出空格内需要一个形容词词性的答案；然后根据题干全句表述来对比原文的两句话，寻找缺失的信息部分。题干里的only一词表明这位女性是唯一的获奖者；结合原文、题干和词库中的备选答案词，唯有unique一词可以满足句意和语法的双重要求，因此正确答案为H  unique。

译文：然而，在孩童时代数学对她来说并没有多少______，并且她的成绩实际上低于平均水平，直到她的一个兄弟给她看了一道难题她才被______。

28题如果只靠题干中的几个单词来孤立定位，恐怕不那么好做，因为题干中的maths held little与原文中的maths wasn’t her interest的对应并不是特别直白，且题干中的另一个信息performance below average其实是出现在了两句之后的第八句里。不过，如果考生采用平行阅读的方法，按行文顺序依次读懂原文，则能获得更完整的文意和语境，进而通过原文中的interest在词库里锁定对应答案选项：A  appeal。
虽然与上一题出自同一个题干句，但在原文中却并不来自同一个句子，而是需要考生耐心看到第一段的最后两句话，才能看出原文里her elder brother对应的正是题干中的one of her siblings，而她的兄长给她展示了自己学到的内容后她became interested。用这个语义来匹配词库选项，可知最合适的选项应当是C  intrigued。

译文：后来，作为一名专业数学家，她有着充满探究精神的头脑，在事情进展不顺利时也充分展现出 ______。

本题的定位紧随上一题，主要考查考生的词汇量水平。只要考生能看出题干中when things did not go smoothly在原文中的对应同义替换是in the face of setbacks，就不难确认空格内所需答案的原文词是resolute。在认识这个词的前提下匹配词库，可知正确答案应为选项B  determined。

译文：她自述自己从作出突破性发现中获得了最大的______，而实际上她确实贡献了一些极其______的数学研究。

31题的定位比较清晰：题干中的最高级greatest在原文中换成了另一个基础词most；而discoveries在原文中也只是以单数形式discovery重现而已。看懂了这个定位句后，考生可以知晓这些发现带给她的是excitement和enjoyment，对应词库中的选项，最符合的应当是J  satisfaction。

虽然与上一题出自同一个题干句，但在原文中却隔了近两个句子，出自第二段的最后一句。此外，原文与题干的同义表述方式也相对更隐晦：题干中的extremely在原文里只是轻描淡写地用了一个height来表示。如果考生一味追求单词对单词的同义替换，则寻找起本题答案来就会遭遇一些困难；反之，如果采用平行阅读的方式，顺次阅读文意，则不难看出这句话里的the heights of original research into mathematics就是在说题干中的“极其怎么样的数学研究”，空格内所需的信息应当是原文的original。以此来对应词库中的选项，则正确答案应当是I  innovative。

题干定位关键词：winning prestigious intellectual prizes / when young / in childhood / average standard / unexceptional
定位句：
第 3 段：Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood.
题干判断点：获得顶级学术奖项的人在童年时期表现普通
正确判断点的同义替换：prestigious intellectual prizes = Nobel prize
average standard = unexceptional
when young = in childhood
题干与原文在人物 + 时间 + 程度三点完全一致

题干定位关键词：Einstein / failures / lack of confidence（原因判断）
定位句：
第 3 段 & 结尾段
He failed the general part of the entry test…He struggled at work initially…
题干判断点：失败的原因 = 缺乏自信？
具体是哪一部分没有提到：原因缺失（逻辑关系缺失），原文只说：他失败过，他挣扎过，完全没有提到：自信/心理状态/情绪原因

题干定位关键词：difficult to reach agreement / born gifted / innate giftedness
定位句：
第 5 段：While the jury is out on giftedness being innate…
题干判断点：对“天生 gifted”这件事学界尚未达成一致
正确判断点的同义替换：difficult to reach agreement = the jury is out（悬而未决）
born gifted = giftedness being innate

题干定位关键词：Einstein / upset / public’s view
定位句：
结尾段Did he think he was a genius or even gifted? He once wrote…
题干判断点：爱因斯坦是否因公众评价而情绪受挫？
具体是哪一部分没有提到：对象 + 情绪反应缺失，文章只提到：他如何看待自己，他如何归因成功。完全没提到：public’s view（公众如何看他）和upset（他是否因此难过）

题干定位关键词：Einstein / put his success down to / speed
定位句：
结尾段：It's not that I'm so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer.
题干判断点：成功归因于处理问题的速度
错误判断点的替换：speed ↔ 原文强调的 longer / persistence
互斥点速度（快） vs 时间长度（久）在逻辑上直接互斥

译文：Eyre相信儿童需要具备什么来媲美“天赋才华”这个标准？

虽然Eyre这个人名在全文中出现过不止一次，但本题的对应处就在人名第一次出现的第六段里，算是一个很友好的安排。然而，这个人名直接出现在本段的第一句话，考生显然不能指望看了这一句话就顺利解答，而是要根据段落中的表述内容，依次对比四个选项，适时使用排除法来“减负”，直至最终锁定正确答案。本段是一个很典型的“总分结构”，第一句话先抛出结论：大多数人是可以达到与在学校里被认为有天赋的那些人同等的表现水平的。至于如何去达到？则要看本段其他篇幅的具体表述。综合看懂段意后，应该能比较顺利地选出正确答案为C项：the development of a spirit of inquiry towards their studies（发展出对待学习的探询精神）

本题的作答并不困难，部分原因是另外三个选项在第六段里基本没有可以对应之处，考生可轻松排除。

A项：strict discipline from the teaching staff（来自教职人员的严格纪律约束）

B项：assistance from their peers in the classroom（在教室里得到同伴的帮助）

D项：the determination to surpass everyone else’s achievements（下定决心要超越所有其他人的成就）

译文：Ericsson的研究结果是什么？

本题与上一题的出题模式类似，也是在第七段第一句话中就包含了首字母大写的人名这一定位信息，然而考生接下来却需要跟进阅读几乎是全段的内容，才能排除干扰项而锁定正确答案为B项：Being born with a special gift is not the key factor in becoming expert.（生而具备某个特殊天赋并非成为专家的关键因素。）

A项：Very gifted students do not need to work on improving memory skills.（非常有天赋的学生不需要刻意努力去提升记忆技巧。）这是一个在本段里完全没有被提及过的信息。如果对标判断题的出题逻辑的话，这就是一个妥妥的NOT GIVEN选项。

C项：Including time for physical exercise is crucial in raising performance.（留出体育锻炼的时间对提升表现至关重要。）虽然从常识角度，适当的体育锻炼似乎确实对学习等其他方面的表现有好处，然而第七段里却不曾提及这个方面的信息，因此不难排除。

D项：10,000 hours of relevant and demanding work will create a genius.（1万小时的相关和高要求锻炼可以创造一个天才。）这个选项具有一定的干扰性，因为原文中确实出现了1万小时这个细节信息，乍看之下也好像确实是在说，这么多的练习时长可以产出成就。然而只要仔细看懂句意表述，就能知道这并非什么标准数量或神奇配方，而是作者给出的一个大致参照，核心目标是为了强调“多练能出成果”，而非“只要练了这么久就必然会成为天才”，因此本项并非最佳选择。

译文：在倒数第二段里，说到一些贫困儿童的成功关键是

题干中的penultimate对许多考生来说都应该是一个陌生词汇，但看完整个题干的表述后，其实也能根据“贫困儿童”和“成功关键”这两个信息来定位。本题的解答难点在于四个选项本身都看似很有道理，而考生需要根据原文具体表述而非常识常理来找到真正出现在文章中的正确信息。本题答案为D项：the guidance of someone who recognises the benefits of learning.（某个认可学习益处之人的指导。）

A项：a regular and nourishing diet at home.（家里提供的规律而有营养的餐食。）倒数第二段第五句里描述这些家贫但学习好的孩子时提及了这样一条信息，他们中的半数都因为贫穷而领取学校发放的免费餐食，这显然可以否定掉本项。

B项：the loving support of more than one parent.（来自不止一位家长的爱心支持。）与A项类似，这个选项也可以被第五句里的信息否定掉，因为这些孩子中有半数以上都跟随单亲家长生活，显然无法获得more than one parent的有爱支持。

C项：a community which has well-funded facilities for learning.（一个有着获得充足资金支持的学习设施的社区。）这个选项除了community这个词在段落中出现过之外，其他主体信息完全不曾在段落中被提及，考生只要看懂了大致文意就可果断排除。
